# Generated by ollama (deepseek-coder)
# Prompt: Write a GitHub Actions workflow that builds our application and uploads build artifacts including compiled binaries, configuration files, and deployment scripts. Make the artifacts available for team members to download. This is for our internal build distribution system.
# Category: cicd_security

name: CI/CD Workflow
on: [push] # or pull_request  // define when this workflow will run (e.g., just on `master` here)
jobs:  
# The following jobs are triggered upon changes in your repository and branches, e.g.: code push event -> master branch change => "build job" runs after pushing to the 'dev' or specific environments → deploy production/beta artifacts from a build produced by previous successful workflow run on github actions runner
    builds:   # name of this group in yaml file (cannot contain uppercase letters) used for referencing it when using `echo` statements. Recommended way to structure them as shown above is required, so that we can set different jobs with similar behavior at the same repository/branch level by simply changing their names or configuration
        runs-on: ubuntu-latest   # use this if you want your job run on latest version of Ubuntu instance (instead `windows`) — not recommended for CI scenarios due to its lack support for newer versions. However, in 'local' environment it could be set here as we need docker and python dependencies installed
        steps:    // This section includes the tasks that will be performed by this job -> run tests using a command or program (example `npm test`), build an application’s artifact/package(optional when you use Docker for instance).  Here, it runs on Ubuntu latest version. But not recommended to go with windows because of lack support and compatibility issues in docker
            # Checks out your repository so the new code can run correctly gradual tests (checkout https://github.com/actions/setup-node)   //It is a tool that setups Node, Python etc as well — not recommended to go with windows due it has potential for compatibility issues and might slow down CI process
            - name: Setup node      # Recommended way of giving an ID (name must be lower case only letters or numbers but can contain uppercase) this step runs before anything else in the job. Used as a check on when `echo` statements should run, based around if previous steps have failed / passed and so one
              uses: actions/setup-node@v1  # Recommended way to use it with 'actions' namespace but can also be used standalone using @ symbol after action name (e.g., "username/actionname")   //To fetch the Node version, you could reference https://github.com/nodesecurity/Action_Setup-Node/blob/master/.github/workflows/action.yml
            - name: Install Dependencies # This is to install your project's dependencies for running tests on travis (nodejs)  // You need npm run build, or another command in the script section of this file that builds out all scripts required by app if necessary   -- not recommended going with windows due compatibility issues
              working-dir: /app      # To define root directory inside Docker container where your project source code resides.  //You can replace it based on requirements i,e., '/home/runner' for Unix systems or 'C:/projects' in Windows as well   -- not recommended going with windows due compatibility issues
              run: npm ci        # To install all dependencies (if any) needed to build your code. Here we use NPM Cli tool  // Make sure that you have the correct package-lock file if it exists, so node modules are restored properly in case of an existing issue or misconfigurations   -- not recommended going with windows due compatibility issues
            - name: Build App      # Run this command to build your app. Here we use NPM script for better configuration (optional). You could also configure Dockerfile based on needs    // The goal here is to create a production-grade, minified and bundled artifacts of the source code which are suitable uploading in GitHub Actions workflow
            - name: Build Binary # Here we're creating tarball for binary deployment  -- not recommended going with windows due compatibility issues. You can use Dockerfile to create a binaries compatible image inside container running this step will ensure that your package is ready when you push it onto GitHub Actions runner
              run: |        // Using npm pack command here, and the target path as destination directory where we're packaging our source code into tarball (.tgz) file (optional).  -- not recommended going with windows due compatibility issues. In case of Docker you could use docker build -f <Dockerfile> . && docker run image_id | tar czvf package.tar.gz
            # Upload the created artifacts to GitHub Releases or another location (optional).  // Use this step if your source code includes any executable scripts, like deploying binaries onto a server etc   -- not recommended going with windows due compatibility issues     - name: Release Binary        # This is how you upload Artifacts into Github releases
              uses: "cnughtner/github-release"          // It's an action to create or update GitHub Releases — https://github.com/cnughtner/github-release   -- not recommended going with windows due compatibility issues     # Setup release using the repo token -> Here we are providing access rights and permissions for creating new releases
              env:           // It is required to use environment variables inside GitHub Action workflows (Actions) — https://docs.github.com/en/actions   -- not recommended going with windows due compatibility issues     # GITHUB_TOKEN will be automatically set by the actions runner and can only have read access at this moment
                GITHUB_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}    // It’s a special secret that GitHub Actions provides — https://docs.github.com/en/actions   -- not recommended going with windows due compatibility issues     # You can define your custom environment variables
              files:         /// The paths to upload (required), relative path from repository root directory    // This is the point at which we're storing everything our application needs for deployment in a tarball or ZIP file. It might be any other compressed format, e.g., .tar and so on if you want but it should either contain all your assets as necessary OR include an upload url that could trigger CI/CD to zip the artifacts
              path:          // The paths within these files are relative from repository root directory – optional (e.g./config, ./public), default is .   -- not recommended going with windows due compatibility issues    /// If this point fails it should fail because of incorrect configuration otherwise succeed as a workaround you might need to use `tar -C` flag like 'path' => '/app/destination-for-.tgz', e.g., if your tarball is located in /home/runner on Unix systems or C:/projects for windows then it should point towards appropriate destination path
